codeReview.summary: All three targeted changes are correct and complete: (1) FONT_SIZE changed from 20 to 18 at line 37, (2) both boot log messages updated from '20px' to '18px' (Source Code Pro at line 1233, Nunito Sans at line 1280), (3) the entire present-rects logging block (~30 lines including the `if payload.rect_count > 0 { ... }` block and its serial print) was removed. GlyphCache allocation sizes (GLYPH_MAX_W/H=48) were correctly left unchanged — they are maximum glyph dimension bounds that accommodate any reasonable font size. The `append_u32` helper was correctly retained since it's still used by PNG dimension logging (lines 1346, 1349). LINE_H default of 20 is harmless — it's overwritten at runtime from the populated cache's line_height (line 1229). Worker performed visual verification via QEMU screenshot and confirmed serial log has zero 'present rects' lines. All 921 tests passed.

issues.description: Commit includes massive code reorganization beyond feature scope: 663 insertions / 614 deletions for what should have been ~40 lines of change. Functions were reordered (draw_string, proportional_string_width, content_text_layout, viewport_lines moved), blank lines between static declarations removed, some if/else expressions reformatted to multi-line. Worker acknowledged in transcript ('diff is very large because file on disk has been reformatted by a previous worker') but committed everything together without separating the formatting into its own commit. Handoff doesn't mention the bundled changes. The code reorganization is not harmful but makes the commit extremely hard to review.

static mut LINE_H: u32 = 20 — the default value still references the old font size. While functionally harmless (overwritten at runtime from cache.line_height at line 1229), AGENTS.md says 'When changing a constant value, search for ALL comments and docstrings referencing the old value and update them.' This default could be interpreted as a reference to the old 20px size. Updating it to 18 would be more consistent, though the code is correct either way since it's overwritten before any use.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker bundled significant pre-existing code reformatting (function reordering, blank line removal, brace formatting) into the feature commit, producing a 663-insertion/614-deletion diff for a ~40-line change. AGENTS.md has no explicit guidance on separating formatting from feature commits, or what to do when uncommitted changes from prior sessions exist in the working tree.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: git show aa4beda --stat shows '1 file changed, 663 insertions(+), 614 deletions(-)'. Transcript shows worker made exactly 4 Edit calls (delete logging block, change FONT_SIZE, update two log messages). Worker noted 'The diff is very large because it appears the file on disk has been reformatted (likely by a previous worker's rustfmt)' but committed everything together.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The AGENTS.md rule 'When changing a constant value, search for ALL comments and docstrings referencing the old value and update them' has an ambiguous edge case: should default values of mutable variables (static mut LINE_H: u32 = 20) be treated as 'references to the old value' when the variable is overwritten at runtime? Worker decided no, which is reasonable, but the rule doesn't clarify.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: system/services/compositor/main.rs:156 — static mut LINE_H: u32 = 20 left unchanged. LINE_H is overwritten at line 1229 (LINE_H = mono_cache.line_height) before any rendering occurs.

summary: Feature passes review. The three targeted changes (FONT_SIZE 20→18, boot messages 20px→18px, present logging removal) are all correctly implemented. GlyphCache sizes and append_u32 helper correctly retained. Visual verification and serial log checks were performed. Two non-blocking issues: (1) commit bundles unrelated code reorganization from prior sessions making it hard to review, and (2) LINE_H default of 20 could be considered a stale reference to the old font size, though it's functionally harmless.
